Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: How do alkali metals differ from alkaline earth metals in atomic structure and general properties?
Answer: The alkali metals all have a single s electron in their outermost shell. In contrast, the alkaline earth metals have a completed s subshell in their outermost shell. In general, the alkali metals react faster and are more reactive than the corresponding alkaline earth metals in the same period.

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Why does the reactivity of the alkali metals decrease from cesium to lithium?

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Answer: $$\text{Na} + \left(\text{I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \text{2NaI} \\ \text{2Na} + \text{Se} \rightarrow \left(\text{Na}\right)_{2} \text{Se} \\ \text{2Na} + \left(\text{O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{Na}\right)_{2} \left(\text{O}\right)_{2}$$ $$\text{Sr} + \left(\text{I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{SrI}\right)_{2} \\ \text{Sr} + \text{Se} \rightarrow \text{SrSe} \\ \text{2Sr} + \left(\text{O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \text{2SrO}$$ $$\text{2Al} + \left(\text{3I}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{2AlI}\right)_{3} \\ \text{2Al} + \text{3Se} \rightarrow \left(\text{Al}\right)_{2} \left(\text{Se}\right)_{3} \\ \text{4Al} + \left(\text{3O}\right)_{2} \rightarrow \left(\text{2Al}\right)_{2} \left(\text{O}\right)_{3}$$

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Predict the best choice in each of the following. You may wish to review the chapter on electronic structure for relevant examples. (a) the most metallic of the elements Al, Be, and Ba (b) the most covalent of the compounds NaCl, CaCl_2, and BeCl_2 (c) the lowest first ionization energy among the elements Rb, K, and Li (d) the smallest among Al, Al^+, and Al^3+ (e) the largest among Cs^+, Ba^2+, and Xe

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Sodium chloride and strontium chloride are both white solids. How could you distinguish one from the other?
Answer: The possible ways of distinguishing between the two include infrared spectroscopy by comparison of known compounds, a flame test that gives the characteristic yellow color for sodium (strontium has a red flame), or comparison of their solubilities in water. At 20 °C, NaCl dissolves to the extent of $$\frac{\text{35.7 g}}{\text{100 mL}}$$ compared with $$\frac{\text{53.8 g}}{\text{100 mL}}$$ for SrCl_2. Heating to 100 °C provides an easy test, since the solubility of NaCl is $$\frac{\text{39.12 g}}{\text{100 mL}} ,$$ but that of SrCl_2 is $$\frac{\text{100.8 g}}{\text{100 mL}} .$$ Density determination on a solid is sometimes difficult, but there is enough difference (2.165 g/mL NaCl and 3.052 g/mL SrCl_2) that this method would be viable and perhaps the easiest and least expensive test to perform.

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: The reaction of quicklime, CaO, with water produces slaked lime, Ca(OH)_2, which is widely used in the construction industry to make mortar and plaster. The reaction of quicklime and water is highly exothermic: $$\text{CaO} ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Ca} ( \text{OH} )_{2} ( s ) \text{Delta} H = \text{unicode[:-]350 kJ} \text{mol}^{\text{unicode[:-]1}}$$ (a) What is the enthalpy of reaction per gram of quicklime that reacts? (b) How much heat, in kilojoules, is associated with the production of 1 ton of slaked lime?

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Write a balanced equation for the reaction of elemental strontium with each of the following: (a) oxygen (b) hydrogen bromide (c) hydrogen (d) phosphorus (e) water
Answer: (a) $$\text{2Sr} ( s ) + \text{O}_{2} ( g ) ⟶ \text{2SrO} ( s ) ;$$ (b) $$\text{Sr} ( s ) + \text{2HBr} ( g ) ⟶ \text{SrBr}_{2} ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} ( g ) ;$$ (c) $$\text{Sr} ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} ( g ) ⟶ \text{SrH}_{2} ( s ) ;$$ (d) $$\text{6Sr} ( s ) + \text{P}_{4} ( s ) ⟶ \text{2Sr}_{3} \text{P}_{2} ( s ) ;$$ (e) $$\text{Sr} ( s ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Sr} ( \text{OH} )_{2} ( a q ) + \text{H}_{2} ( g )$$

(Assume that all the mercury from the fish ends up as mercury(II) chloride in the body and that a fatal dose is 0.20 g of HgCl_2.) How many pounds of fish is this?
Answer: 11 lb

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Does metallic tin react with HCl?
Answer: Yes, tin reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce hydrogen gas.

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: What is tin pest, also known as tin disease?

Topic: 18.1 Periodicity
Question: Compare the nature of the bonds in PbCl_2 to that of the bonds in PbCl_4.
Answer: In PbCl_2, the bonding is ionic, as indicated by its melting point of 501 °C. In PbCl_4, the bonding is covalent, as evidenced by it being an unstable liquid at room temperature.

Question: Write an equation for the reduction of cesium chloride by elemental calcium at high temperature.
Answer: $$\text{2CsCl} ( l ) + \text{Ca} ( g ) \overset{ \left ( \left .\begin{matrix}{a}\text{countercurrent} \\ \text{fractionating} \\ \text{tower}\end{matrix}\right . \right ) }{ \left ( \vec{} \right ) } \text{2Cs} ( g ) + \text{CaCl}_{2} ( l )$$

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Why is it necessary to keep the chlorine and sodium, resulting from the electrolysis of sodium chloride, separate during the production of sodium metal?

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Give balanced equations for the overall reaction in the electrolysis of molten lithium chloride and for the reactions occurring at the electrodes. You may wish to review the chapter on electrochemistry for relevant examples.
Answer: Cathode (reduction): $$\text{2Li}^{+} + \text{2e}^{-} ⟶ \text{2Li} ( l ) \text{;}$$ Anode (oxidation): $$\text{2Cl}^{-} ⟶ \text{Cl}_{2} ( g ) + \text{2e}^{\text{unicode[:-]}} ;$$ Overall reaction: $$\text{2Li}^{+} + \text{2Cl}^{-} ⟶ \text{2Li} ( l ) + \text{Cl}_{2} ( g )$$

Question: What mass, in grams, of hydrogen gas forms during the complete reaction of 10.01 g of calcium with water?

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: How many grams of oxygen gas are necessary to react completely with 3.01 $$\times$$ 10^21 atoms of magnesium to yield magnesium oxide?

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Magnesium is an active metal; it burns in the form of powder, ribbons, and filaments to provide flashes of brilliant light. Why is it possible to use magnesium in construction?
Answer: Despite its reactivity, magnesium can be used in construction even when the magnesium is going to come in contact with a flame because a protective oxide coating is formed, preventing gross oxidation. Only if the metal is finely subdivided or present in a thin sheet will a high-intensity flame cause its rapid burning.

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Why is it possible for an active metal like aluminum to be useful as a structural metal?

Topic: 18.2 Occurrence and Preparation of the Representative Metals
Question: Describe the production of metallic aluminum by electrolytic reduction.
Answer: Extract from ore: $$\text{AlO} ( \text{OH} ) ( s ) + \text{NaOH} ( a q ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Na} [ \text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{4} ] ( a q )$$ Recover: $$\text{2Na} [ \text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{4} ] ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{SO}_{4} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{2Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) + \text{Na}_{2} \text{SO}_{4} ( a q ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l )$$ Sinter: $$\text{2Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) ⟶ \text{Al}_{2} \text{O}_{3} ( s ) + \text{3H}_{2} \text{O} ( g )$$ Dissolve in Na_3AlF_6( l ) and electrolyze: $$\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{3e}^{-} ⟶ \text{Al} ( s )$$

Question: What is the common ore of tin and how is tin separated from it?

Topic: 18.3 Structure and General Properties of the Metalloids
Question: Describe the hybridization of silicon and the molecular structure of the following molecules and ions: (a) (CH_3)_3SiH (b) $$\text{SiO}_{4} ^{4-}$$ (c) Si_2H_6 (d) Si(OH)_4 (e) $$\text{SiF}_{6} ^{2-}$$
Answer: (a) (CH_3)_3SiH: sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (b) $$\text{SiO}_{4} ^{\text{4unicode[:-]}} :$$ sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (c) Si_2H_6: sp ^3 bonding about each Si; the structure is linear along the Si-Si bond; (d) Si(OH)_4: sp ^3 bonding about Si; the structure is tetrahedral; (e) $$\text{SiF}_{6} ^{\text{2unicode[:-]}} :$$ sp ^3 d ^2 bonding about Si; the structure is octahedral

Topic: 18.3 Structure and General Properties of the Metalloids
Question: Why is boron limited to a maximum coordination number of four in its compounds?
Answer: Boron has only s and p orbitals available, which can accommodate a maximum of four electron pairs. Unlike silicon, no d orbitals are available in boron.

Topic: 18.3 Structure and General Properties of the Metalloids
Question: Suppose you discovered a diamond completely encased in a silicate rock. How would you chemically free the diamond without harming it?
Answer: A mild solution of hydrofluoric acid would dissolve the silicate and would not harm the diamond.

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Carbon forms a number of allotropes, two of which are graphite and diamond. Silicon has a diamond structure. Why is there no allotrope of silicon with a graphite structure?

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Nitrogen in the atmosphere exists as very stable diatomic molecules. Why does phosphorus form less stable P_4 molecules instead of P_2 molecules?
Answer: In the N_2 molecule, the nitrogen atoms have an σ bond and two π bonds holding the two atoms together. The presence of three strong bonds makes N_2 a very stable molecule. Phosphorus is a third-period element, and as such, does not form π bonds efficiently; therefore, it must fulfill its bonding requirement by forming three σ bonds.

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Determine the oxidation number of each element in each of the following compounds: (a) HCN (b) OF_2 (c) AsCl_3
Answer: (a) H = 1+, C = 2+, and N = 3-; (b) O = 2+ and F = 1-; (c) As = 3+ and Cl = 1-

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Determine the oxidation state of sulfur in each of the following: (a) SO_3 (b) SO_2 (c) $$\text{SO}_{3} ^{2-}$$

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Arrange the following in order of increasing electronegativity: F; Cl; O; and S.
Answer: S < Cl < O < F

Topic: 18.4 Structure and General Properties of the Nonmetals
Question: Why does white phosphorus consist of tetrahedral P_4 molecules while nitrogen consists of diatomic N_2 molecules?

Topic: 18.5 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Hydrogen
Question: Why does hydrogen not exhibit an oxidation state of 1- when bonded to nonmetals?
Answer: The electronegativity of the nonmetals is greater than that of hydrogen. Thus, the negative charge is better represented on the nonmetal, which has the greater tendency to attract electrons in the bond to itself.

Topic: 18.5 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Hydrogen
Question: The reaction of calcium hydride, CaH_2, with water can be characterized as a Lewis acid-base reaction: $$\text{CaH}_{2} ( s ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Ca} ( \text{OH} )_{2} ( a q ) + \text{2H}_{2} ( g )$$ Identify the Lewis acid and the Lewis base among the reactants. The reaction is also an oxidation-reduction reaction. Identify the oxidizing agent, the reducing agent, and the changes in oxidation number that occur in the reaction.

Topic: 18.5 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Hydrogen
Question: In drawing Lewis structures, we learn that a hydrogen atom forms only one bond in a covalent compound. Why?
Answer: Hydrogen has only one orbital with which to bond to other atoms. Consequently, only one two-electron bond can form.

Topic: 18.5 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Hydrogen
Question: What mass of CaH_2 is necessary to react with water to provide enough hydrogen gas to fill a balloon at 20 °C and 0.8 atm pressure with a volume of 4.5 L? The balanced equation is: $$\text{CaH}_{2} ( s ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{Ca} ( \text{OH} )_{2} ( a q ) + \text{2H}_{2} ( g )$$

Topic: 18.6 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Carbonates
Question: Complete and balance the following chemical equations: (a) hardening of plaster containing slaked lime $$\text{Ca} ( \text{OH} )_{2} + \text{CO}_{2} ⟶$$ (b) removal of sulfur dioxide from the flue gas of power plants $$\text{CaO} + \text{SO}_{2} ⟶$$ (c) the reaction of baking powder that produces carbon dioxide gas and causes bread to rise $$\text{NaHCO}_{3} + \text{NaH}_{2} \text{PO}_{4} ⟶$$
Answer: (a) $$\text{Ca} ( \text{OH} )_{2} ( a q ) + \text{CO}_{2} ( g ) ⟶ \text{CaCO}_{3} ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ;$$ (b) $$\text{CaO} ( s ) + \text{SO}_{2} ( g ) ⟶ \text{CaSO}_{3} ( s ) ;$$ (c) $$\text{2NaHCO}_{3} ( s ) + \text{NaH}_{2} \text{PO}_{4} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{Na}_{3} \text{PO}_{4} ( a q ) + \text{2CO}_{2} ( g ) + \text{2H}_{2} \text{O} ( l )$$

Answer: (a) NH^2-: (b) N_2F_4: (c) $$\text{NH}_{2} ^{\text{unicode[:-]}} :$$ (d) NF_3: (e) $$\text{N}_{3} ^{\text{unicode[:-]}} :$$

Topic: 18.7 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Nitrogen
Question: Explain how ammonia can function both as a Brønsted base and as a Lewis base.
Answer: Ammonia acts as a Brønsted base because it readily accepts protons and as a Lewis base in that it has an electron pair to donate. Brønsted base: $$\text{NH}_{3} + \text{H}_{3} \text{O}^{+} ⟶ \text{NH}_{4} ^{+} + \text{H}_{2} \text{O}$$ Lewis base: $$\text{2NH}_{3} + \text{Ag}^{+} ⟶ \text{[H}_{3} \text{N} - \text{Ag} - \text{NH}_{3} ]^{+}$$

Topic: 18.7 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Nitrogen
Question: For each of the following, draw the Lewis structure, predict the ONO bond angle, and give the hybridization of the nitrogen. You may wish to review the chapters on chemical bonding and advanced theories of covalent bonding for relevant examples. (a) NO_2 (b) $$\text{NO}_{2} ^{-}$$ (c) $$\text{NO}_{2} ^{+}$$
Answer: (a) NO_2: Nitrogen is sp ^2 hybridized. The molecule has a bent geometry with an ONO bond angle of approximately 120°. (b) $$\text{NO}_{2} ^{\text{unicode[:-]}} :$$ Nitrogen is sp ^2 hybridized. The molecule has a bent geometry with an ONO bond angle slightly less than 120°. (c) $$\text{NO}_{2} ^{\text{+}} :$$ Nitrogen is sp hybridized. The molecule has a linear geometry with an ONO bond angle of 180°.

Topic: 18.7 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Nitrogen
Question: Although PF_5 and AsF_5 are stable, nitrogen does not form NF_5 molecules. Explain this difference among members of the same group.
Answer: Nitrogen cannot form a NF_5 molecule because it does not have d orbitals to bond with the additional two fluorine atoms.

Topic: 18.8 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Phosphorus
Question: Phosphoric acid, one of the acids used in some cola drinks, is produced by the reaction of phosphorus(V) oxide, an acidic oxide, with water. Phosphorus(V) oxide is prepared by the combustion of phosphorus. (a) Write the empirical formula of phosphorus(V) oxide. (b) What is the molecular formula of phosphorus(V) oxide if the molar mass is about 280. (c) Write balanced equations for the production of phosphorus(V) oxide and phosphoric acid. (d) Determine the mass of phosphorus required to make 1.00 $$\times$$ 10^4 kg of phosphoric acid, assuming a yield of 98.85%.

Question: Predict the product of burning francium in air.
Answer: FrO_2

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Write balanced chemical equations for the following reactions: (a) zinc metal heated in a stream of oxygen gas (b) zinc carbonate heated until loss of mass stops (c) zinc carbonate added to a solution of acetic acid, CH_3CO_2H (d) zinc added to a solution of hydrobromic acid
Answer: (a) $$\text{2Zn} ( s ) + \text{O}_{2} ( g ) ⟶ \text{2ZnO} ( s ) ;$$ (b) $$\text{ZnCO}_{3} ( s ) ⟶ \text{ZnO} ( s ) + \text{CO}_{2} ( g ) ;$$ (c) $$\text{ZnCO}_{3} ( s ) + 2 \text{CH}_{3} \text{COOH} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{Zn} ( \text{CH}_{3} \text{COO} )_{2} ( a q ) + \text{CO}_{2} ( g ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ;$$ (d) $$\text{Zn} ( s ) + \text{2HBr} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{ZnBr}_{2} ( a q ) + \text{H}_{2} ( g )$$

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Write balanced chemical equations for the following reactions: (a) cadmium burned in air (b) elemental cadmium added to a solution of hydrochloric acid (c) cadmium hydroxide added to a solution of acetic acid, CH_3CO_2H

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Illustrate the amphoteric nature of aluminum hydroxide by citing suitable equations.
Answer: $$\text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) + 3 \text{H}^{\text{+}} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{Al}^{3+} + 3 \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ;$$ $$\text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{3} ( s ) + \text{OH}^{-} ⟶ [ \text{Al} ( \text{OH} )_{4} ]^{\text{unicode[:-]}} ( a q )$$

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Write balanced chemical equations for the following reactions: (a) metallic aluminum burned in air (b) elemental aluminum heated in an atmosphere of chlorine (c) aluminum heated in hydrogen bromide gas (d) aluminum hydroxide added to a solution of nitric acid

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Write balanced chemical equations for the following reactions: (a) sodium oxide added to water (b) cesium carbonate added to an excess of an aqueous solution of HF (c) aluminum oxide added to an aqueous solution of HClO_4 (d) a solution of sodium carbonate added to solution of barium nitrate (e) titanium metal produced from the reaction of titanium tetrachloride with elemental sodium
Answer: (a) $$\text{Na}_{2} \text{O} ( s ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ⟶ \text{2NaOH} ( a q ) ;$$ (b) $$\text{Cs}_{2} \text{CO}_{3} ( s ) + \text{2HF} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{2CsF} ( a q ) + \text{CO}_{2} ( g ) + \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ;$$ (c) $$\text{Al}_{2} \text{O}_{3} ( s ) + 6 \text{HClO}_{4} ( a q ) ⟶ \text{2Al} ( \text{ClO}_{4} )_{3} ( a q ) + 3 \text{H}_{2} \text{O} ( l ) ;$$ (d) $$\text{Na}_{2} \text{CO}_{3} ( a q ) + \text{Ba} ( \text{NO}_{3} )_{2} ( a q ) ⟶ 2 \text{NaNO}_{3} ( a q ) + \text{BaCO}_{3} ( s ) ;$$ (e) $$\text{TiCl}_{4} ( l ) + \text{4Na} ( s ) ⟶ \text{Ti} ( s ) + \text{4NaCl} ( s )$$

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: What volume of 0.250 M H_2SO_4 solution is required to neutralize a solution that contains 5.00 g of CaCO_3?

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Which is the stronger acid, HClO_4 or HBrO_4? Why?
Answer: HClO_4 is the stronger acid because, in a series of oxyacids with similar formulas, the higher the electronegativity of the central atom, the stronger is the attraction of the central atom for the electrons of the oxygen(s). The stronger attraction of the oxygen electron results in a stronger attraction of oxygen for the electrons in the O-H bond, making the hydrogen more easily released. The weaker this bond, the stronger the acid.

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction of an excess of oxygen with each of the following. Remember that oxygen is a strong oxidizing agent and tends to oxidize an element to its maximum oxidation state. (a) Mg (b) Rb (c) Ga (d) C_2H_2 (e) CO

Topic: 18.9 Occurrence, Preparation, and Compounds of Oxygen
Question: Which is the stronger acid, H_2SO_4 or H_2SeO_4? Why? You may wish to review the chapter on acid-base equilibria.
Answer: As H_2SO_4 and H_2SeO_4 are both oxyacids and their central atoms both have the same oxidation number, the acid strength depends on the relative electronegativity of the central atom. As sulfur is more electronegative than selenium, H_2SO_4 is the stronger acid.

Topic: 18.10 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Sulfur
Question: Oxygen forms double bonds in O_2, but sulfur forms single bonds in S_8. Why?
Answer: Sulfur is able to form double bonds only at high temperatures (substantially endothermic conditions), which is not the case for oxygen.

Topic: 18.11 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Halogens
Question: Why is SnCl_4 not classified as a salt?
Answer: SnCl_4 is not a salt because it is covalently bonded. A salt must have ionic bonds.

Topic: 18.11 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of Halogens
Question: Which is the stronger acid, HClO_3 or HBrO_3? Why?
Answer: In oxyacids with similar formulas, the acid strength increases as the electronegativity of the central atom increases. HClO_3 is stronger than HBrO_3; Cl is more electronegative than Br.

Topic: 18.12 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of the Noble Gases
Question: A mixture of xenon and fluorine was heated. A sample of the white solid that formed reacted with hydrogen to yield 81 mL of xenon (at STP) and hydrogen fluoride, which was collected in water, giving a solution of hydrofluoric acid. The hydrofluoric acid solution was titrated, and 68.43 mL of 0.3172 M sodium hydroxide was required to reach the equivalence point. Determine the empirical formula for the white solid and write balanced chemical equations for the reactions involving xenon.
Answer: The empirical formula is XeF_6, and the balanced reactions are: $$\\ \\ \text{Xe} \left(\right. g \left.\right) + \left(\text{3F}\right)_{2} \left(\right. g \left.\right) \overset{ \Delta }{\rightarrow} \left(\text{XeF}\right)_{6} \left(\right. s \left.\right) \\ \left(\text{XeF}\right)_{6} \left(\right. s \left.\right) + \left(\text{3H}\right)_{2} \left(\right. g \left.\right) \rightarrow \text{6HF} \left(\right. g \left.\right) + \text{Xe} \left(\right. g \left.\right)$$

Topic: 18.12 Occurrence, Preparation, and Properties of the Noble Gases
Question: Basic solutions of Na_4XeO_6 are powerful oxidants. What mass of Mn(NO_3)_2•6H_2O reacts with 125.0 mL of a 0.1717 M basic solution of Na_4XeO_6 that contains an excess of sodium hydroxide if the products include Xe and solution of sodium permanganate?
